---
date: "2026-07-27"
---

## What's Clever

- Part 7 — four clever, transferable moves

::: notes
Welcome back to Chapter six-point-two, part seven of nine. In part six we set the reading order. Now we name what is clever about the paper — four moves, each one transferable to your own capstone, not just admirable in this one.
:::

## Letting Firms Define Their Own Industry

- The deepest idea is a refusal
- Reject the government's top-down taxonomy
- Let *firms* say who they are, in their own words
- Let the *data* draw the boundaries
- Pattern: when an official label is convenient but wrong, build your own

::: notes
The first move is the deepest, and it is a refusal. Rather than accept the government's top-down taxonomy, Hoberg and Phillips let firms say who they are, in their own words, and let the data draw the boundaries. The pattern generalizes: when an official classification is convenient but wrong, build your own from a primary source the classifiers ignored. The ten-K product description sat in plain sight — legally mandated, machine-readable — for decades. They were the ones who read it as data.
:::

## A Network, Not A Partition

- SIC's three defects: one box per firm, frozen, no overlaps
- TNIC fixes all three — *because* it's built from pairwise similarity
- A weighted network replaces disjoint sets
- The right object for competition: overlapping, shifting rivalries
- Sense when you need a *network*, not a *partition*

::: notes
The second move: firm-specific, time-varying, intransitive networks. S-I-C's three defects — one box per firm, frozen in time, and no overlaps — are exactly what T-N-I-C fixes, and it fixes them because it is built from pairwise similarity rather than a partition. This is a different mathematical object — a weighted network — replacing a cruder one, disjoint sets. And it is the right object for competition, which really is a web of overlapping, shifting rivalries. Recognizing when your phenomenon needs a network and not a partition is a modeling instinct worth stealing.
:::

## The Cosine Bridge, And A Public Good

- Whole apparatus reduces to one operation: the cosine
- Chapter 1.2's cosine doesn't care if vectors hold returns or words
- That generality launches the AI module, Chapter 6.5
- Publishing the **TNIC library** made the measure a public good
- Infrastructure compounds: every paper built on it is evidence for it

::: notes
The third move: cosine similarity as the bridge from words to numbers. The whole apparatus reduces to one operation you already knew — the cosine between two vectors. Chapter one-point-two said correlation is a cosine; this paper shows the cosine does not care whether the vectors hold returns or word counts. That generality is why this paper launches the A-I module in Chapter six-point-five: modern language models represent text as vectors and compare them with cosine similarity, so the embedding you will meet there is the grown-up cousin of the bag-of-words vector you build in the notebook. The fourth move is the most consequential, and it is sociological. By publishing the T-N-I-C library, the authors made their measure a public good — hundreds of later papers use these industries without re-deriving them, the way you use Fama-French factors without re-sorting portfolios. Infrastructure compounds: every paper built on it is, indirectly, evidence for it. Designing a capstone, ask not only "what do I find?" but "could what I build be useful to the next person?"
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Four transferable moves: **let firms define their own industry** from a primary source; model competition as a **network, not a partition**; use the **cosine** as the universal bridge from words to numbers; and **publish the measure** so it becomes reusable infrastructure that compounds.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part seven: four clever, transferable moves. Let firms define their own industry from a primary source the classifiers ignored. Model competition as a network, not a partition, because overlapping shifting rivalries demand it. Use the cosine as the universal bridge from words to numbers — the same operation that launches the A-I module. And publish the measure as reusable infrastructure, so it compounds across hundreds of later papers. In part eight we turn the skepticism around and ask what is vulnerable.
:::
